reasons: Belerick punishes repeated basic attacks and multi-unit pressure, which fits Sun's clone-heavy damage pattern.
Sun and his doppelgangers can increase Belerick's return-damage value in grouped fights.

proof.summary: Sun wants to overwhelm enemies with repeated clone attacks, while Belerick punishes attackers through durable frontline presence and return-damage pressure.
worksBestWhen: Belerick stands between Sun and the backline.
Belerick has defensive items before Sun's split-push pressure becomes too large.
failureCases: Sun avoids teamfights and wins side-lane macro instead.
Belerick is forced to chase Sun instead of protecting allies.

reasons: X.Borg's area damage helps clear clones and pressure the real Sun at the same time.
True-damage threat and AoE pressure reduce the value of Sun's extended clone fight.

proof.summary: Sun wants prolonged multi-unit melee pressure, while X.Borg can hit the area and punish grouped clone patterns with true-damage threat.
worksBestWhen: X.Borg fights around minion waves or objective zones where Sun's clones must group.
X.Borg has armor uptime before committing.
failureCases: Sun splits the map and avoids direct AoE fights.
X.Borg loses Firaga Armor before Sun commits.

reasons: Baxia can reduce Sun's healing value from clone damage and durable side-lane trades.
His tank profile helps absorb repeated attacks while the team identifies the real Sun.

proof.summary: Sun benefits from sustained doppelganger pressure and recovery, while Baxia reduces healing value and holds frontline space.
worksBestWhen: Baxia stays with the team instead of chasing clones alone.
Baxia applies anti-heal pressure before Sun's extended trade begins.
failureCases: Sun plays split-push and avoids Baxia.
Baxia cannot protect damage dealers from side entry.

reasons: Ruby can use area crowd control and sustain-oriented skills to fight through Sun's clone pressure.
Sun's clones can make Ruby's AoE control more valuable in grouped fights.

proof.summary: Sun wants clone confusion and sustained melee pressure, while Ruby can control multiple nearby units and sustain through skill hits.
worksBestWhen: Ruby fights near allies who can identify and burst the real Sun.
Ruby uses AoE control after Sun summons clones.
failureCases: Ruby lacks anti-heal or damage follow-up.

reasons: Alpha can fight in sustained melee and apply area damage, making him less confused by clone pressure than single-target heroes.
Beta's true-damage follow-up helps pressure Sun during extended trades.

proof.summary: Sun wants long clone-based trades, while Alpha has AoE sustain and true-damage follow-up for extended melee fights.
worksBestWhen: Alpha hits Sun and clones together.
Alpha has anti-heal support or teammate burst.
failureCases: Sun avoids direct fighting and wins split-push rotations.
Alpha misses key skills and cannot sustain.
